beats.narration: Sooner or later someone hands you a part that isn't metal: a rubber lining, a glass window. Ultrasound can test it. Here's how.

payload.title: BEYOND METALS
payload.sub: Testing rubber, glass, and the parts no code quite covers.

beats.narration: Three numbers decide the technique. Velocity sets the time base. Impedance sets coupling and reflection. Attenuation caps your range and frequency.
The classic job: checking a rubber lining's bond from the steel side. Bonded rubber drains energy, so echoes die fast. A disbond rings on.

payload.heading: The lining bond test: ring-down
payload.note: FAST DECAY = BONDED · LONG RING = DISBOND

beats.narration: Glass flips the script: no grains, almost no attenuation. Run ten to twenty five megahertz and catch tiny seeds, stones, and surface cracks.
So when the material is strange and the code is silent, start with three numbers: velocity, impedance, attenuation. Then the technique designs itself.

payload.sub: Three numbers — the technique designs itself.
